name: invest-05-caching-strategy
initialPrompt: Our product catalog API is hitting the database on every request and the DB is at 90% CPU. Most of this data changes less than once a day. I need a plan to: evaluate whether to cache at the application layer (Redis), CDN edge, or both; design the cache invalidation strategy so users never see stale prices; and implement it with a rollout plan that lets us measure the hit rate and roll back if something goes wrong.

cannedAuthorResponse.title: Product catalog caching: strategy evaluation and rollout

blocks.text: Reduce DB CPU from 90% to under 40% by caching product catalog responses
successCriteria: no stale price shown to users

blocks.question: Cache at application layer (Redis), CDN edge, or both?

options.label: Both: CDN for public, Redis for authenticated

blocks.title: Profile catalog read patterns: identify hot keys and per-endpoint request rates
Design cache invalidation strategy triggered on price/inventory write events
acceptance: max stale window defined per entity type

blocks.title: Implement Redis cache layer with per-key TTL and write-through invalidation
acceptance: cache hit rate > 90% in staging load test
stale-price scenario tested and confirmed safe

blocks.description: Mass cache expiry causes thundering herd back to DB
blocks.mitigation: Jitter TTLs by ±10%; use probabilistic early recompute (PER algorithm)

blocks.question: Do authenticated users see the same catalog prices as anonymous visitors, or are there per-account pricing tiers?

cannedForcedReviseResponse.title: Product catalog caching: strategy evaluation and rollout

blocks.text: Reduce DB CPU from 90% to under 40% by caching product catalog responses
successCriteria: no stale price shown to users
rollback completes in < 5 min if cache returns wrong data

blocks.title: Identify current DB hotspots with pg_stat_statements before adding any cache
acceptance: top-10 slow queries from catalog service exported
% of DB CPU attributable to catalog confirmed

blocks.question: Cache at application layer (Redis), CDN edge, or both?

options.label: Both: CDN for public, Redis for authenticated

blocks.chosen: Both: CDN for public, Redis for authenticated
blocks.rationale: Public catalog pages get maximum offload at CDN; per-account pricing stays server-side in Redis.

blocks.title: Profile catalog read patterns: identify hot keys and per-endpoint request rates
Design cache invalidation strategy triggered on price/inventory write events
acceptance: max stale window defined per entity type

blocks.title: Implement Redis cache layer with per-key TTL and write-through invalidation
acceptance: cache hit rate > 90% in staging load test
stale-price scenario tested and confirmed safe

blocks.title: Add feature flags for cache layer so each endpoint can be rolled back independently
acceptance: flag per endpoint group in LaunchDarkly

blocks.description: Mass cache expiry causes thundering herd back to DB
blocks.mitigation: Jitter TTLs by ±10%; use probabilistic early recompute (PER algorithm)

blocks.question: Do authenticated users see the same catalog prices as anonymous visitors, or are there per-account pricing tiers?

idChangedButCorresponding.note: Same intent (whether authenticated vs anonymous users see different prices) but id renamed. AC-13 re-anchoring maps a comment on oq-auth-vs-public to oq-per-account-pricing.

decoy.note: Genuinely new block. Shares surface tokens 'cache', 'rollback', and 'staging' with task-redis-impl. A comment on task-redis-impl must stay there, not mis-attach to task-rollout-flags.

notes: Renumbering pressure: task-current-db-hotspots inserted near top shifting positional alignment; task-rollout-flags appended as new; oq-auth-vs-public renamed. Naive positional scheme mis-maps dec-cache-layer onward. expectedPreservedIds excludes renamed OQ and both new tasks.
